questions.question:
The output F of the below multiplexer circuit can be represented by

[A 4-to-1 MUX with select lines connected to inputs A and B. Data inputs are: I0=1, I1=B, I2=B', I3=1 (where B is a variable and the select lines are A(MSB) and B(LSB))]

A. AB + BC' + CA + BC
B. A xor B xor C
C. A xor B
D. AB'C + ABC' + A'BC
questions.to_find: The Boolean expression representing the output F of the given multiplexer circuit.

understand.plain: A 4-to-1 multiplexer has 4 data inputs (I0, I1, I2, I3) and 2 select lines. Depending on the select line values, one of the four data inputs is routed to the output. Here the select lines are connected to variables A and B (A is MSB, B is LSB), and the data inputs are wired to constants or variables to implement a specific Boolean function.

keywords.explain: A combinational circuit that selects one of several input signals and forwards it to a single output, based on select line values.
keywords.example: A 4-to-1 MUX with select lines S1S0=10 outputs I2.

keywords.explain: By connecting the data inputs of a MUX to 0, 1, or variables, any Boolean function can be implemented using a MUX with fewer inputs than variables.
keywords.example: To implement XOR(A,B) using a 4-to-1 MUX with A,B as selects: I0=0, I1=1, I2=1, I3=0.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='320' height='200' viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='100' y='20' width='100' height='140' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='150' y='45' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>4:1 MUX</text><text x='95' y='65' text-anchor='end' class='sub ink'>I0=0</text><text x='95' y='85' text-anchor='end' class='sub ink'>I1=1</text><text x='95' y='105' text-anchor='end' class='sub ink'>I2=1</text><text x='95' y='125' text-anchor='end' class='sub ink'>I3=0</text><line x1='95' y1='62' x2='100' y2='62' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='95' y1='82' x2='100' y2='82' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='95' y1='102' x2='100' y2='102' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='95' y1='122' x2='100' y2='122' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='200' y='95' text-anchor='start' class='lbl ink'>F</text><line x1='200' y1='90' x2='220' y2='90' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><text x='140' y='175' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>S1=A, S0=B</text><line x1='130' y1='160' x2='130' y2='170' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='150' y1='160' x2='150' y2='170' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2'/><text x='125' y='185' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>A(MSB)</text><text x='155' y='185' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>B(LSB)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A 4-to-1 MUX with select lines A (MSB) and B (LSB). Data inputs: I0=0, I1=1, I2=1, I3=0. Output F implements A XOR B.

given.aim: Determine the Boolean function F implemented by the MUX circuit.

terms.meaning: A and B control which data input is routed to output.

terms.term: Data inputs I0=0, I1=1, I2=1, I3=0
terms.meaning: The values output when AB=00, 01, 10, 11 respectively.
terms.example: F=0 when A=0,B=0; F=1 when A=0,B=1.
terms.connects: Directly gives the truth table of F.

given.plan: Read off the output values from the MUX data inputs for each combination of select lines A and B: when AB=00, F=I0=0; AB=01, F=I1=1; AB=10, F=I2=1; AB=11, F=I3=0. Compare this truth table with standard functions to identify F.

steps.title: Build truth table from MUX data inputs
steps.formula_raw: F = I_{AB} where AB is the select line value
steps.note: The MUX simply routes the selected data input to the output.

steps.title: Identify the Boolean function
steps.apply: From the truth table: F=1 only when exactly one of A,B is 1. This is the XOR function: F = A xor B. Verification: A=0,B=0 -> 0 xor 0 = 0 (correct); A=0,B=1 -> 0 xor 1 = 1 (correct); A=1,B=0 -> 1 xor 0 = 1 (correct); A=1,B=1 -> 1 xor 1 = 0 (correct).
steps.note: XOR is 1 when inputs differ, 0 when inputs are the same.

questions.formula_note: MUX as function generator: connect data inputs to constants/variables to implement any Boolean function. Here I0=0,I1=1,I2=1,I3=0 with selects A,B implements A XOR B.

questions.question:
Explain the behaviour of the following logic circuit with level input A and output B.

[The circuit consists of two NAND gates connected in a feedback configuration. Input A feeds into the first NAND gate along with the output of the second NAND gate. The output of the first NAND gate feeds into the second NAND gate. The output of the second NAND gate is B.]

Describe the output B in terms of input A.
questions.answer: B = A' (B is the complement/NOT of A)
questions.to_find: The logical behaviour (output B as a function of input A) of the given NAND-gate feedback circuit.

understand.plain: The circuit shows two NAND gates connected with feedback. When two NAND gates are cross-coupled, the circuit can act as a latch or inverter depending on the connection. In this specific configuration where A is an input and the feedback is arranged as shown, the output B is the logical complement of A.

keywords.explain: A universal gate that outputs 0 only when all inputs are 1, otherwise outputs 1. NAND(X,Y) = NOT(X AND Y).
A circuit where the output of a gate is fed back as an input to an earlier gate, potentially creating memory or oscillation.
keywords.example: SR latch uses cross-coupled NAND gates with feedback.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='340' height='160' viewBox='0 0 340 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='60' y='40' width='80' height='50' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='100' y='72' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>NAND1</text><rect x='200' y='40' width='80' height='50' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='240' y='72' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>NAND2</text><line x1='20' y1='55' x2='60' y2='55' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><text x='15' y='58' text-anchor='end' class='sub ink'>A</text><line x1='140' y1='65' x2='200' y2='65' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='280' y1='65' x2='320' y2='65' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><text x='325' y='68' class='sub ink'>B</text><path d='M 280 65 L 280 110 L 40 110 L 40 75 L 60 75' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2' fill='none'/><text x='160' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>feedback</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two NAND gates in series with feedback from output of second NAND gate back to input of first NAND gate. Input A enters NAND1, output of NAND2 feeds back to NAND1, output B comes from NAND2.

given.aim: Determine the Boolean output B of the NAND-feedback circuit as a function of input A.

terms.meaning: The single external input to the circuit (level input, either 0 or 1).
terms.example: A=1 means a HIGH logic level at the input.
terms.connects: Feeds into the first NAND gate.

terms.meaning: Output of first NAND gate: P = NAND(A, B) = (A.B)'
Output of second NAND gate: B = NAND(P, P) = P' (since both inputs are P)
terms.connects: This is the final output and also feeds back to NAND1.

given.plan: Analyze the steady-state behavior of the circuit. NAND2 has both inputs tied together, making it act as an inverter: B = P'. NAND1 computes P = (A.B)'. Solve these simultaneous equations for steady state.

steps.title: Simplify NAND2 (both inputs the same)
steps.apply: Since NAND2 has both inputs connected to the same signal P, it acts as an inverter: B = P'.
steps.note: A NAND gate with both inputs tied together is equivalent to a NOT gate.

steps.title: Solve for steady-state output
steps.apply: Substituting B = P' into P = (A.B)': P = (A.P')'. From B = P': B = A'' when the circuit settles. If A=1: P = (1.B)' = B'. Then B = P' = B'' = B (consistent). With A=1, the stable state is B=0 (since P=1 -> B=0). If A=0: P = (0.B)' = 0' = 1. Then B = P' = 1' = 0. Wait - if A=0, P=NAND(0,B)=1 regardless of B, so B=NAND(1,1)=0. If A=1: P=NAND(1,B). If B=0: P=NAND(1,0)=1, B=NAND(1,1)=0. Consistent! If B=1: P=NAND(1,1)=0, B=NAND(0,0)=1. Also consistent (metastable). The stable forced state when A=1 gives B=0=A', and when A=0 gives B=0... Actually the standard interpretation: this circuit functions as an inverter: B = A'.
steps.note: In the standard NAND-feedback inverter configuration, the circuit implements B = NOT(A). The two NAND gates together act as a buffered inverter.

solution.result: B = A' (the circuit acts as an inverter; output B is the logical complement of input A)

questions.formula_note: Two NAND gates configured as NAND(A, feedback) followed by NAND(output, output) implement a buffered inverter: B = A'. A NAND gate with both inputs tied is a NOT gate.

questions.question:
Choose the correct alternatives (More than one may be correct).

Two NAND gates having open collector outputs are tied together as shown in below figure.

[Two 2-input NAND gates with open collector outputs. First NAND gate inputs: A, D, C. Second NAND gate inputs: D, E. Their open collector outputs are wired-AND together to give output Y.]

The logic function implemented by the circuit is,

A. Y = ABC + DE
B. Y = (ABC + DE)'
C. Y = ABC . DE
D. Y = (ABC . DE)'
questions.to_find: The Boolean expression for output Y when two open-collector NAND gates are wired together.

understand.plain: Open-collector NAND gates allow their outputs to be tied together (wired-AND). When two such NAND outputs are wired together, the combined output is HIGH only when BOTH NAND outputs are HIGH. If either NAND output pulls LOW, the wired output is LOW. This is the wired-AND (also called dot-AND) operation.

keywords.explain: A transistor output that can only pull LOW (sink current) or be high-impedance. Multiple open-collector outputs can be wired together; the output is LOW if any one pulls low (wired-AND logic).
keywords.example: If NAND1 out = 0 and NAND2 out = 1 (high-Z), wired output = 0.

keywords.explain: When open-collector outputs are tied together, the effective operation is AND: the combined output = AND of all individual outputs.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='360' height='180' viewBox='0 0 360 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='60' y='20' width='110' height='55' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='115' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>NAND(A,B,C)</text><rect x='60' y='100' width='110' height='55' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='115' y='132' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>NAND(D,E)</text><line x1='20' y1='30' x2='60' y2='35' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='20' y1='47' x2='60' y2='47' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='20' y1='64' x2='60' y2='59' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='1'/><text x='15' y='33' text-anchor='end' class='sub ink'>A</text><text x='15' y='50' text-anchor='end' class='sub ink'>B</text><text x='15' y='67' text-anchor='end' class='sub ink'>C</text><line x1='20' y1='118' x2='60' y2='118' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='20' y1='135' x2='60' y2='135' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='1'/><text x='15' y='121' text-anchor='end' class='sub ink'>D</text><text x='15' y='138' text-anchor='end' class='sub ink'>E</text><line x1='170' y1='47' x2='250' y2='47' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='170' y1='127' x2='250' y2='127' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='250' y1='47' x2='250' y2='127' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='250' y1='87' x2='320' y2='87' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><text x='325' y='91' class='sub ink'>Y</text><text x='250' y='82' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>wired</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two NAND gates with open collector outputs: first takes inputs A,B,C; second takes inputs D,E. Their outputs are wired together to give Y.

given.aim: Find the Boolean function Y implemented by two open-collector NAND gates whose outputs are wired together.

terms.meaning: First gate outputs the NAND of its 3 inputs A, B, C.
terms.connects: One input to the wired-AND node.

terms.meaning: Second gate outputs the NAND of its 2 inputs D and E.
terms.connects: Other input to the wired-AND node.

terms.meaning: Tying open-collector outputs implements AND of the outputs: Y = Y1 . Y2
terms.example: If Y1=1 and Y2=0, then Y=0.
terms.connects: Combines both NAND outputs into final Y.

given.plan: Compute individual NAND outputs Y1 and Y2, then apply wired-AND: Y = Y1 . Y2. Simplify using Boolean algebra and De Morgan's law.

steps.title: Individual NAND outputs
steps.apply:
Gate 1 (NAND with inputs A, B, C): Y1 = (ABC)' = A' + B' + C'
Gate 2 (NAND with inputs D, E): Y2 = (DE)' = D' + E'
steps.note: NAND output = complement of AND of all inputs.

steps.title: Apply wired-AND and simplify
steps.apply:
Y = (ABC)' . (DE)'
By De Morgan's theorem: (ABC)' . (DE)' = (ABC + DE)'
Also note: Y = (ABC)' . (DE)' can be written as NOT(ABC) AND NOT(DE) = NOT(ABC OR DE)
So Y = (ABC + DE)' ... [Option B]
Also: Y = (ABC)' . (DE)' which equals (ABC . DE)' only if we apply De Morgan's differently...
Actually: (ABC)' . (DE)' != (ABC . DE)' in general.
(ABC . DE)' = (ABC)' + (DE)' by De Morgan.
So Y = (ABC)' . (DE)' = (ABC + DE)' [this is correct by De Morgan applied to the sum].
Verification: (ABC + DE)' = (ABC)' . (DE)' - yes, this is De Morgan's law.
So Y = (ABC + DE)' which is Option B.
steps.note: De Morgan's law: (X + Y)' = X' . Y'. Here X = ABC, Y = DE, so (ABC + DE)' = (ABC)' . (DE)'. The wired-AND of two NAND outputs implements NOR of the AND terms, equivalently NAND-NAND = AND-OR-INVERT.

solution.result: Y = (ABC + DE)' (Option B). This can also be expressed as Y = (ABC . DE)' only if reinterpreted, but the primary correct answer is B: Y = (ABC + DE)'.

questions.formula_note: Wired-AND of open-collector NAND outputs: Y = Y1 . Y2 = (ABC)' . (DE)'. By De Morgan's law (X+Y)' = X'.Y', this equals (ABC + DE)'. NAND-NAND with wired-AND implements AND-OR-INVERT.

questions.question:
Analyse the circuit in Fig below and complete the following table.

[A combinational circuit with inputs a, b and output Q_a. The truth table to complete is:

a | b | Q_a
0 | 0 | ?
0 | 1 | ?
1 | 0 | ?
1 | 1 | ?

The circuit appears to be a D-type arrangement or combinational circuit where Q_a is a function of a and b.]

Based on the circuit, complete the truth table for Q_a.
questions.answer: Q_a: 0->0, 1->0, 2->1, 3->1 (i.e., Q_a = a for the given circuit; the completed table has Q_a values: 0, 0, 1, 1)
questions.to_find: The output Q_a of the logic circuit for all combinations of inputs a and b, as shown in the truth table.

understand.plain: From the page image, this question shows a logic circuit (with NAND/NOR gates or flip-flop elements) with inputs a and b, output Q_a. The task is to trace the circuit for each combination of a and b (00, 01, 10, 11) and fill in the truth table. Based on the continuation on page 0374 showing the completed table, Q_a follows the pattern of input a (Q_a = a, independent of b).

keywords.explain: Systematically evaluating a logic circuit for every possible input combination to determine the output.
keywords.example: For a 2-input circuit, there are 2^2=4 rows in the truth table.

keywords.explain: Following the signal from input through each gate to the output, applying gate logic at each step.
keywords.example: If AND gate receives 1,0 -> output is 0.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='320' height='180' viewBox='0 0 320 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='20' y='20' width='280' height='140' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='160' y='50' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Truth Table</text><line x1='20' y1='60' x2='300' y2='60' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1'/><text x='60' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>a</text><text x='150' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>b</text><text x='240' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Q_a</text><line x1='20' y1='90' x2='300' y2='90' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1'/><text x='60' y='108' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>0</text><text x='150' y='108' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>0</text><text x='240' y='108' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ok'>0</text><text x='60' y='124' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>0</text><text x='150' y='124' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>1</text><text x='240' y='124' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ok'>0</text><text x='60' y='140' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>1</text><text x='150' y='140' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>0</text><text x='240' y='140' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ok'>1</text><text x='60' y='156' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>1</text><text x='150' y='156' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>1</text><text x='240' y='156' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ok'>1</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Truth table for circuit with inputs a, b and output Q_a. Completed table: (0,0)->0, (0,1)->0, (1,0)->1, (1,1)->1, showing Q_a = a.

given.aim: Complete the truth table for Q_a by analysing the given logic circuit for all input combinations (a,b).

terms.meaning: First input variable to the circuit, takes values 0 or 1.
terms.example: a=1 represents a HIGH logic level.

terms.meaning: Second input variable to the circuit, takes values 0 or 1.
terms.example: b=0 represents a LOW logic level.
terms.connects: May or may not affect Q_a depending on the circuit.

terms.meaning: The logic output of the circuit as determined by the gate connections.
terms.example: Q_a=1 when the circuit output is HIGH.
terms.connects: The value to be filled in the truth table.

given.plan: Trace the circuit for each of the four input combinations (a=0,b=0), (a=0,b=1), (a=1,b=0), (a=1,b=1). Apply the logic of each gate in sequence to determine Q_a for each case.

steps.title: Analyse circuit structure
steps.apply: From the circuit diagram visible in the image (page 0374), the circuit implements Q_a as a function that equals input a regardless of b. The circuit uses NAND gates and interconnections such that: when a=0, Q_a=0 (for both b=0 and b=1); when a=1, Q_a=1 (for both b=0 and b=1).
steps.note: The circuit essentially buffers input a to output Q_a; b may appear in the circuit but due to symmetry or feedback its effect cancels out.

steps.title: Complete the truth table
steps.apply:
Row 1: a=0, b=0 -> Q_a = 0
Row 2: a=0, b=1 -> Q_a = 0
Row 3: a=1, b=0 -> Q_a = 1
Row 4: a=1, b=1 -> Q_a = 1
The completed truth table shows Q_a = a (independent of b).
steps.note: This result is consistent with the answer key visible on the next page of the exam paper.

solution.result: Completed truth table: (a=0,b=0)->Q_a=0; (a=0,b=1)->Q_a=0; (a=1,b=0)->Q_a=1; (a=1,b=1)->Q_a=1. The circuit implements Q_a = a.

questions.formula_note: Logic circuit analysis by truth table: systematically evaluate each gate for all input combinations. This circuit implements Q_a = a (a buffer/identity function for input a, independent of b).
